prompt: A detailed copperplate scientific engraving in the style of 17th-century Age of Exploration cartographic illustrations. The image depicts a three-dimensional curved mathematical surface rendered entirely through precise cross-hatching and stipple engraving techniques — denser hatching in steep regions, sparse hatching on flat plateaus. The surface has multiple valleys and one deep global minimum. A single small polished brass sphere sits on the surface, with a dotted trajectory path behind it showing its descent from a high point down through a saddle point, past a shallow local minimum, and arriving near the deepest global minimum. Fine compass-drawn concentric contour lines surround each minimum like topographic elevation lines on an antique map. The engraving style uses varying line weights: bold outlines for the surface edge, medium cross-hatching for shading, and fine stipple dots for the flattest regions. Aged cream-colored paper with subtle foxing. No text, no labels, no numbers, no equations, no modern elements. Purely a scientific copper engraving study of a mathematical terrain.
tradition_name: Scientific copperplate engraving from the Age of Exploration
tradition_approach: Cross-hatching line density encodes gradient steepness, stipple dots mark flat loss plateaus, compass-drawn contour lines map loss surface topology, and the brass sphere's dotted trajectory traces the optimization path — all using copperplate engraving's actual tonal vocabulary.
